_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). General Medical: Shock. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Shock

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Shock
points: Initiate early fluid resuscitation and vasopressors to maintain/restore adequate perfusion to vital organs
Differentiate between possible underlying causes of shock to promptly initiate additional therapy

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Signs of poor perfusion (due to a medical cause) such as one or more of the following: a. Altered mental status
Potential etiologies of shock: a. Hypovolemic (hemorrhagic or non-hemorrhagic)
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Shock due to suspected trauma [See Trauma Section]

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Supine position with legs elevated (Trendelenburg) unless respiratory compromise
Hemorrhage control: direct pressure, tourniquet, wound packing
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98%
Prevent heat loss: cover patient to maintain normothermia
CPR/AED for cardiac arrest from refractory shock

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: IV access maintenance (non-medicated) if existing access
12-lead ECG acquisition for cardiogenic shock
CPAP for cardiogenic shock with pulmonary edema component
Epinephrine IM auto-injector for anaphylactic shock
Albuterol SVN for anaphylactic shock with bronchospasm

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access: 2 large-bore IVs when possible
IV fluid resuscitation: NS or LR 250-500 mL boluses for distributive/hypovolemic shock (adult); 20 mL/kg pediatric
Epinephrine IV/IO 0.1-0.5 mg for anaphylactic shock
IV/IO naloxone 0.4-2 mg for opioid-induced distributive shock
Blood glucose monitoring and correction
Waveform capnography for hemodynamic monitoring

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Vasopressors for refractory distributive shock (septic/neurogenic): norepinephrine 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min IV preferred
Dopamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min IV for cardiogenic shock with bradycardia
Epinephrine infusion 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min IV for anaphylactic or refractory shock
Corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 125 mg IV) for anaphylactic or adrenal shock
Needle decompression bilaterally for obstructive shock from tension pneumothorax
Pericardiocentesis authorization (per medical direction) for cardiac tamponade
Fluid resuscitation strategy: permissive hypotension (MAP ≥50) for hemorrhagic shock until surgical control
Tranexamic acid (TXA) 1 g IV over 10 min within 3 hours of injury for hemorrhagic shock
RSI and intubation for airway compromise in shock
12-lead ECG interpretation for cardiogenic cause (STEMI, arrhythmia)

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Recognition of cardiogenic shock - If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Early, aggressive IV fluid administration is essential in the treatment of suspected septic shock
Patients predisposed to shock: a. Immunocompromised (patients undergoing chemotherapy or with a primary or acquired immunodeficiency)

In most adults, tachycardia is the first sign of compensated shock, and may persist for hours. Tachycardia can be a late sign of shock in children and a tachycardic child may be close to cardiovascular collapse
Hypotension indicates uncompensated shock, which may progress to cardiopulmonary failure within minutes. Hypotension is a late and ominous sign in pediatric uncompensated shock

Hydrocortisone succinate, if available, is preferred over methylprednisolone and dexamethasone for the patient with adrenal insufficiency because of its dual glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid effects a. Patients with no reported history of adrenal axis dysfunction may have adrenal suppression due to their acute illness, and hydrocortisone should be considered for any patient showing signs of treatment-resistant shock

Decreased perfusion manifested by altered mental status, or abnormalities in capillary refill or pulses, decreased urine output (1 mL/kg/hr): a. Cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive shock: capillary refill greater than 2 seconds, diminished peripheral pulses, mottled cool extremities

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Medications administered
' Full vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment) with reassessment q 15 minutes or more frequently as appropriate
' Lactate level (if available)
' Neurologic status assessment [See Appendix VII. Neurologic Status Assessment]
' Amount of fluids given

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Recognition of cardiogenic shock - If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration
Potential etiologies of shock: a. Hypovolemic (hemorrhagic or non-hemorrhagic)
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
